Rise Academy
DSA Foundations
Lesson 11
Introduction
01What is DSA?
02Why DSA Matters
Complexity
03Complexity Analysis
04Big O Notation
05Space vs Time
Data Structures
06Arrays
07Strings
08Linked Lists
09Stacks
10Queues
Progress 1/10
Lesson 01

What is DSA?

"DSA — Data Structures and Algorithms — is the study of how to organise data and the step-by-step methods we use to process it."

Analogy

Think of a supermarket. The shelves are a data structure — they organise products so you can find them quickly. Your shopping strategy is an algorithm — whether you zigzag randomly or follow the aisle layout systematically determines how efficiently you finish. Same goal, very different performance.

The Two Halves of DSA

fig. 01 — Data Structure vs Algorithm
Fig. 1 — A data structure organises information; an algorithm is the recipe that acts on it

The Core Concepts

What is a Data Structure?

A data structure is a way of organising and storing data in memory so that it can be accessed and modified efficiently. It is not about the data itself — it is about the container and the rules of that container. An array stores items in a numbered sequence. A linked list chains items together with pointers. A hash map pairs keys to values for near-instant lookup. Each structure is a different promise about how the data will behave when you interact with it.

Choosing the right data structure is often the most important engineering decision in a program. Use the wrong one and a simple operation becomes needlessly expensive. Use the right one and complex problems can feel almost trivial.

What is an Algorithm?

An algorithm is a finite, ordered sequence of well-defined instructions that transforms an input into a desired output. The word comes from the name of the 9th-century Persian mathematician Muhammad ibn Musa al-Khwarizmi. Crucially, an algorithm must always terminate — it cannot run forever — and for the same input it must always produce the same output.

Algorithms are language-agnostic. The same binary search algorithm can be implemented in Python, JavaScript, Rust, or written in pseudocode. The language is a detail. The logic is the algorithm.

Why They Are Inseparable

Data structures and algorithms are two sides of the same coin. An algorithm operates on a data structure — and the choice of data structure determines which algorithms are efficient and which are not. You cannot analyse an algorithm in isolation without knowing what it is operating on. Binary search is O(log n) only on a sorted array. It becomes meaningless on a linked list. Understanding both together is what gives you real power as an engineer.

Four Qualities of a Good Algorithm

Correctness

It must produce the right output for every valid input, including edge cases like empty arrays, single elements, or duplicate values.

Efficiency

It should use the minimum necessary time and memory. An algorithm that works but takes 10 minutes is not useful in production.

Clarity

Another engineer should be able to read and understand it without a long explanation. Clever code that is hard to read is a liability.

Finiteness

It must terminate for any valid input. An algorithm that could run indefinitely is not an algorithm — it is an infinite loop waiting to happen.

Pseudocode

Pseudocode
// Find the maximum number in a list FUNCTION findMax(list): IF list is empty: RETURN null max ← list[0] // assume first element is the max FOR EACH element in list: IF element > max: max ← element // found a new maximum RETURN max // Time: O(n) — must check every element // Space: O(1) — only one extra variable (max)

Implementation

JavaScript 
/**
       * findMax — Returns the largest number in an array.
       * Time:  O(n) — single pass through every element
       * Space: O(1) — only a single variable regardless of input size
       */
      function findMax(arr) {
        if (arr.length === 0) return null;   // guard: empty input
      
        let max = arr[0];                     // step 1: assume first is largest
      
        for (let i = 1; i < arr.length; i++) { // step 2: walk the array
          if (arr[i] > max) {               // step 3: update if bigger
            max = arr[i];
          }
        }
      
        return max;                          // step 4: return the winner
      }
      
      // ── Usage ──────────────────────────────────────────────
      const scores  = [42, 7, 98, 31, 55, 6];
      const highest = findMax(scores);
      console.log(highest); // 98
      
      console.log(findMax([]));         // null  — empty array
      console.log(findMax([-5, -1, -8])); // -1   — all negative
      console.log(findMax([3]));         // 3    — single element
TypeScript 
/**
       * findMax — Returns the largest number in an array.
       * Time:  O(n) — single pass through every element
       * Space: O(1) — only a single variable regardless of input size
       */
      function findMax(arr: number[]): number | null {
        if (arr.length === 0) return null;   // guard: empty input
      
        let max: number = arr[0];             // step 1: assume first is largest
      
        for (let i: number = 1; i < arr.length; i++) {
          if (arr[i] > max) {               // step 2: update if bigger
            max = arr[i];
          }
        }
      
        return max;
      }
      
      // ── Usage ──────────────────────────────────────────────
      const scores: number[] = [42, 7, 98, 31, 55, 6];
      const highest: number | null = findMax(scores);
      console.log(highest); // 98
      
      // TypeScript catches misuse at compile time:
      // findMax(['a', 'b']); // Error: string[] not assignable to number[]

Complexity of findMax

Operation Time Space Why
Access arr[0] O(1) O(1) Direct index access, no traversal needed
Single comparison O(1) O(1) One operation, independent of input size
Full findMax pass O(n) O(1) Must visit every element once; only stores max
Best case (max is first) O(n) O(1) Still must verify no larger element exists later

Where You'll See This in the Real World

Google Search

Inverted index (data structure) + PageRank (algorithm) together retrieve the most relevant result from billions of pages in milliseconds.

GPS Routing

A graph (data structure) represents roads and junctions. Dijkstra's algorithm (algorithm) finds the shortest path between any two nodes.

Social Media Feed

A priority queue (data structure) holds posts ranked by engagement score. A ranking algorithm re-orders them each time you refresh.

Bank Transactions

A B-tree (data structure) indexes millions of records. A ledger reconciliation algorithm verifies balance consistency across distributed systems.

Lesson 02

Why DSA Matters

"The difference between a program that works and a program that scales is almost always a question of data structures and algorithms."

Analogy

Picture two cashiers. The first checks every item against a handwritten list — scanning line by line until they find a match. That's O(n). The second scans a barcode and gets an instant database lookup — same result, regardless of how large the inventory is. That's O(1). With 10 items, both cashiers seem fine. With 10,000 items, only one can keep the queue moving.

Growth Rates at a Glance

fig. 02 — How different Big O complexities grow as n increases
Fig. 2 — At n=1,000, the difference between O(1) and O(2ⁿ) is the difference between instant and heat-death-of-the-universe

Why This Is Not Academic

What Happens at Scale

When you have 10 users, almost any code works. A nested loop that checks every user against every other user runs 100 operations — imperceptible. Scale to 10,000 users and that same loop runs 100,000,000 operations. Your server is now melting for no reason other than a bad algorithmic choice made when the dataset was small.

This is not a theoretical problem. It is the most common reason why startups hit a wall after their first viral moment. The code that got you to product-market fit will betray you the moment it needs to handle real load. DSA is what you reach for when that moment arrives.

The Knight Capital Story

In August 2012, Knight Capital Group — one of the largest US equity dealers — lost $440 million in 45 minutes due to a software deployment error that activated a dormant trading algorithm. The algorithm began executing thousands of erroneous trades per second. Because there was no circuit breaker — no algorithmic check on the rate and volume of operations — the system ran unchecked until human operators physically shut it down.

This is an extreme example, but it illustrates that algorithms are not just about elegance. They are about control, predictability, and knowing exactly what your code will do under every condition. Engineers who deeply understand algorithms build systems that fail gracefully. Engineers who don't build systems that fail expensively.

Watch Out

An O(n²) algorithm on a dataset of 1 million records will perform 1 trillion operations. At 1 billion operations per second, that is 16 minutes for a single query. The same operation with an O(n log n) algorithm takes under 20 milliseconds. The code "works" either way. Only one of them works in production.

Why Tech Companies Test DSA in Interviews

The technical interview — with its whiteboard problems and Big O questions — is often criticised as disconnected from daily work. In many ways it is. But the underlying reason it exists is sound: companies with billion-user scale cannot afford engineers who write working-but-slow code. When a single backend endpoint serves 50,000 requests per second, an O(n²) function that takes 10ms on your laptop takes 10 seconds at that scale. DSA interviews are a proxy test for "can this person reason about what their code will do under load?"

Working Code vs Efficient Code

The most important mindset shift in becoming a senior engineer is moving from "does it work?" to "does it work, at scale, with minimal resources?" A junior engineer celebrates when tests pass. A senior engineer asks: what is the time complexity of this solution? Can it be improved? What happens if this collection grows to 10 million items? DSA is the vocabulary and the tools that make those conversations possible.

Pseudocode — O(n) vs O(1) Lookup

Pseudocode
// SLOW: check every item in a list — O(n) FUNCTION slowContains(list, target): FOR EACH item in list: IF item == target: RETURN true RETURN false // 1,000 items → up to 1,000 checks // 1,000,000 items → up to 1,000,000 checks FUNCTION fastContains(hashSet, target): RETURN hashSet.has(target) // 1 check — always, regardless of size → O(1)

Implementation — Slow vs Fast Lookup

JavaScript 
// ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
      // SLOW: Linear search through an array — O(n)
      // Every additional item = one more potential check
      // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
      function slowContains(list, target) {
        for (let i = 0; i < list.length; i++) {
          if (list[i] === target) return true;
        }
        return false;
      }
      
      // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
      // FAST: Set lookup — O(1)
      // JavaScript Set uses a hash table internally.
      // .has() is always one operation, regardless of size.
      // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
      function fastContains(set, target) {
        return set.has(target);
      }
      
      // ── Benchmark comparison ────────────────────────────────
      const N = 1_000_000;
      const bigArray = Array.from({ length: N }, (_, i) => i);
      const bigSet   = new Set(bigArray);
      const target   = 999_999;            // worst case: last element
      
      console.time('array O(n)');
      slowContains(bigArray, target);     // scans up to 1,000,000 items
      console.timeEnd('array O(n)');     // ~5–12ms
      
      console.time('set O(1)');
      fastContains(bigSet, target);       // always 1 hash lookup
      console.timeEnd('set O(1)');       // ~0.001ms
TypeScript 
// ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
      // SLOW: Linear search through an array — O(n)
      // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
      function slowContains<T>(list: T[], target: T): boolean {
        for (let i: number = 0; i < list.length; i++) {
          if (list[i] === target) return true;
        }
        return false;
      }
      
      // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
      // FAST: Set lookup — O(1)
      // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
      function fastContains<T>(set: Set<T>, target: T): boolean {
        return set.has(target);
      }
      
      // ── Benchmark comparison ────────────────────────────────
      const N: number = 1_000_000;
      const bigArray: number[] = Array.from({ length: N }, (_, i) => i);
      const bigSet: Set<number> = new Set(bigArray);
      const target: number = 999_999;
      
      console.time('array O(n)');
      slowContains(bigArray, target);     // scans up to 1,000,000 items
      console.timeEnd('array O(n)');     // ~5–12ms
      
      console.time('set O(1)');
      fastContains(bigSet, target);       // always 1 hash lookup
      console.timeEnd('set O(1)');       // ~0.001ms

The Real Cost at Scale

Complexity n = 10 n = 1,000 n = 1,000,000 Verdict
O(1) 1 op 1 op 1 op Always instant
O(log n) 3 ops 10 ops 20 ops Excellent
O(n) 10 ops 1,000 ops 1,000,000 ops Acceptable
O(n²) 100 ops 1,000,000 ops 10¹² ops Dangerous at scale
O(2ⁿ) 1,024 ops 10³⁰¹ ops 10³⁰¹⁰⁰⁰ ops Unusable
Key Insight

At n=1,000, O(n²) performs one million operations. At 1 billion ops/sec, that is 1ms — still fine. But at n=100,000, it is 10 billion operations — 10 seconds per request. One order-of-magnitude growth in data causes a thousand-fold slowdown. This is why scale-up is the moment bad algorithmic choices become catastrophic.

Where You'll See This in the Real World

Ride-hailing Dispatch

Matching riders to drivers across a city requires spatial data structures (k-d trees) and greedy algorithms — brute-force pairing would be impossibly slow.

Database Indexing

Without an index, every query is O(n). A B-tree index makes the same query O(log n) — the entire concept of database performance rests on this trade-off.

Video Streaming Buffer

Adaptive bitrate algorithms (like used by Netflix) continuously select optimal video quality using time-series data — O(n) scan on every frame would stall playback.

Compiler Optimisation

A compiler analyses your code's data flow graph to eliminate redundant operations. It applies algorithmic transformations to make your program faster before it ever runs.

Lesson 03

Complexity Analysis

"Complexity analysis measures algorithm efficiency in terms of operations relative to input size — not in seconds or milliseconds."

Analogy

Two runners race a marathon. Runner A has newer shoes and runs on a faster track — but uses a terrible route. Runner B has older gear — but took a masterclass in optimal routing. At 5km, A is ahead. At 42km, B wins by an hour. A faster machine running a slow algorithm always loses to a slower machine running a brilliant one — at scale.

Growth Rate Curves

fig. 03 — As n grows, how fast do operations grow?
Fig. 3 — Four fundamental growth patterns — the gap between them widens dramatically as n increases

How Complexity Analysis Works

Why Not Just Measure in Seconds?

Timing an algorithm in seconds is machine-dependent. Your laptop runs the same function three times faster than a Raspberry Pi and ten times slower than a data-centre server. The measurement changes every time you upgrade your hardware, switch languages, or run the code under different system load. It tells you nothing portable or predictable.

Complexity analysis instead counts abstract operations — comparisons, assignments, index accesses — and expresses how that count grows relative to the input size. This number is machine-independent, language-independent, and holds true forever.

What "n" Means

The variable n represents the size of the input your algorithm operates on. If you pass an array of 100 elements, n = 100. If you pass 1,000,000 elements, n = 1,000,000. The goal of complexity analysis is to describe the relationship between n and the number of operations your algorithm performs. Does doubling n double the work? Square it? Leave it unchanged? That relationship is the complexity.

Key Insight

n is not always an array length. For a string, n is the number of characters. For a graph, n might be the number of nodes or edges. For a matrix, n might be rows × columns. Always ask: what is the primary input dimension this algorithm scales with?

Best, Worst, and Average Case

Any given algorithm can behave differently depending on the structure of its input. Consider searching for a value in an unsorted array. If the target is the very first element, you find it in one step — that is the best case. If it is the last element or not present at all, you check every element — that is the worst case. The average case assumes random distribution and calculates the expected number of steps across all possible inputs.

Best Case — Ω (Omega)

The minimum number of operations under the most favourable input. Rarely the focus — best case is almost never guaranteed in production.

Worst Case — O (Big O)

The maximum operations under the most unfavourable input. This is what we design and optimise for. If the worst case is acceptable, everything else is.

Average Case — Θ (Theta)

Expected operations averaged across all valid inputs. Useful for probabilistic analysis but harder to calculate. Quicksort is O(n²) worst but O(n log n) average.

Why We Always Design for the Worst Case

In systems that serve real users, you do not get to choose which inputs arrive. A malicious actor, an unexpected data shape, or simply a large dataset can all trigger worst-case behaviour at any moment. Designing for average case is designing for a world where everything goes well — which is not the world software runs in.

When a senior engineer says "this function is O(n)," they mean its worst-case behaviour is linear. That guarantee is what lets you reason safely about how a system will behave under load.

Pseudocode — Linear Search with Operation Counts

Pseudocode
FUNCTION linearSearch(arr, target): i ← 0 // 1 operation — assignment WHILE i < arr.length: // n+1 comparisons (loop condition) IF arr[i] == target: // n comparisons (worst case) RETURN i // 1 operation — best case exit i ← i + 1 // n operations — increment RETURN -1 // 1 operation — not found // Total: 3n + 3 operations in worst case // Drop constants and coefficients → O(n) // Space: O(1) — no extra data structures used

Implementation

JavaScript 
/**
       * linearSearch — Find the index of target in arr.
       *
       * Complexity breakdown:
       *   - Initialise i:       1 op
       *   - Loop condition:     n+1 comparisons
       *   - Element comparison: n comparisons (worst case)
       *   - Increment i:        n additions
       *   Total: 3n + 2 → O(n) time, O(1) space
       */
      function linearSearch(arr, target) {
        for (let i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) { // n iterations worst case
          if (arr[i] === target) {              // O(1) per comparison
            return i;                           // best case: O(1) if target is arr[0]
          }
        }
        return -1;                              // not found
      }
      
      // ── Cases ───────────────────────────────────────────────
      const data = [4, 11, 2, 88, 7, 3, 42];
      
      // Best case  — O(1): target is at index 0
      console.log(linearSearch(data, 4));   // 0  — 1 comparison
      
      // Worst case — O(n): target is last or absent
      console.log(linearSearch(data, 42));  // 6  — 7 comparisons
      console.log(linearSearch(data, 99));  // -1 — 7 comparisons
      
      // We say the function is O(n) because Big O
      // always describes the WORST case.
TypeScript 
/**
       * linearSearch — Find the index of target in arr.
       * Time: O(n) | Space: O(1)
       */
      function linearSearch<T>(arr: T[], target: T): number {
        for (let i: number = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
          if (arr[i] === target) return i;
        }
        return -1;
      }
      
      // Generic version works on any comparable type
      const nums: number[] = [4, 11, 2, 88, 7];
      const words: string[] = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry'];
      
      console.log(linearSearch(nums,  88));        // 3
      console.log(linearSearch(words, 'banana')); // 1
      
      // TypeScript enforces type consistency — prevents:
      // linearSearch(nums, 'hello'); // Error: string not assignable to number

linearSearch — Case Analysis

CaseTimeSpaceWhen it happens
Best case O(1) O(1) Target is the very first element
Average case O(n/2) → O(n) O(1) Target at random position — we drop the ½ constant
Worst case O(n) O(1) Target is last element or not present at all

Where You'll See This in the Real World

Database Query Planning

PostgreSQL's query planner performs complexity analysis on every query to decide whether to use a sequential scan O(n) or index scan O(log n).

React Render Optimisation

React's reconciliation algorithm is O(n) by design — it compares the virtual DOM in a single pass. Earlier approaches were O(n³) and made large UIs unusable.

API Rate Limiting

A sliding-window rate limiter must check request counts in O(1) or O(log n). An O(n) check per request would itself become the bottleneck under load.

Compiler Analysis

Compilers analyse the complexity of loops and inline or unroll code based on whether the loop is O(1) (constant iterations) or O(n) — automatic optimisation at build time.

Lesson 04

Big O Notation

"Big O notation is the universal language engineers use to describe how an algorithm's resource usage grows as input size grows."

Analogy

Imagine searching for a book in a library. O(1) — you know the exact shelf and slot, you walk straight to it. O(log n) — you use the card catalogue: open to the middle, go left or right, halve again, find it in a few steps. O(n) — the catalogue is broken, you walk every aisle checking each spine. O(n²) — you compare every book against every other book to find duplicates. Same library, wildly different costs.

The Big O Growth Chart

fig. 04 — All major complexity classes on one chart
Fig. 4 — The gap between complexity classes is not a gradual slope — it is an exponential cliff

Understanding Big O

What Does the "O" Stand For?

The "O" stands for Ordnung, the German word for "order of magnitude." It was introduced by the mathematician Paul Bachmann in 1894. Big O notation gives us the order of growth of a function — not its exact value, but the shape of how it scales. O(n²) does not mean exactly n² operations. It means that as n grows, the operations grow proportionally to n².

How to Read O(n²) Aloud

Say: "Order n squared" or "Big O of n squared". Never "O n 2" or "oh en squared". The phrasing matters in interviews and team discussions because it signals you understand it as a growth relationship, not a formula. Similarly: O(log n) is "order log n", O(1) is "order 1" or "constant time".

The Three Rules of Big O

Rule 1 — Drop Constants

O(2n) simplifies to O(n). O(500) simplifies to O(1). Constants are irrelevant because we care about growth shape, not exact counts. A constant factor never changes the order of magnitude.

Rule 2 — Drop Non-Dominant Terms

O(n² + n) simplifies to O(n²). At large n, the n term becomes negligible next to n². Keep only the term that grows fastest — it will dominate all others.

Rule 3 — Different Inputs, Different Variables

A function that loops through array A then array B is O(a + b), not O(n). Use different variable names for different inputs. Only combine them if the arrays are the same input.

How to Calculate Big O by Counting Loops

The fastest way to estimate complexity is to count your loops. A single loop over n elements is O(n). A loop inside a loop, both running over n elements, is O(n²). Three nested loops: O(n³). A loop that halves the problem each iteration (like binary search) is O(log n). Each loop you add multiplies the complexity; each halving adds a log factor.

Pro Tip

When you see code that loops n times inside a function that is called n times, the total is O(n²) — even if the outer repetition is not an explicit loop. Watch for recursive calls, map/filter chains, and nested array methods hiding quadratic complexity.

Pseudocode — O(n) vs O(n²)

Pseudocode
// ── O(n) — one loop ───────────────────────────── FUNCTION printAll(list): FOR EACH item in list: // runs n times PRINT item // O(1) each → total O(n) // ── O(n²) — loop inside a loop ────────────────── FUNCTION printPairs(list): FOR EACH a in list: // runs n times FOR EACH b in list: // runs n times for each a PRINT a, b // O(1) each → total n × n = O(n²) // With n=1,000: printAll → 1,000 ops // printPairs → 1,000,000 ops

Implementation — Four Complexity Classes

JavaScript 
// ── O(1) — Constant Time ──────────────────────────────────
      // Does exactly the same number of operations regardless of n.
      function getFirst(arr) {
        return arr[0];  // 1 operation always — index access is O(1)
      }
      getFirst([1, 2, 3]);              // 1 op
      getFirst(new Array(1_000_000)); // still 1 op
      
      // ── O(n) — Linear Time ───────────────────────────────────
      // One operation per element. Doubles when n doubles.
      function sumAll(arr) {
        let total = 0;
        for (let i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) { // loops n times
          total += arr[i];                        // O(1) per iteration
        }
        return total;
      }
      
      // ── O(n²) — Quadratic Time ───────────────────────────────
      // Every element compared to every other element.
      // Watch for this pattern — it is the most common bug at scale.
      function printPairs(arr) {
        for (let i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {      // n iterations
          for (let j = 0; j < arr.length; j++) {    // n iterations each
            console.log(arr[i], arr[j]);            // n × n = n² total
          }
        }
      }
      
      // ── O(log n) — Logarithmic Time ──────────────────────────
      // Halves the search space each iteration.
      // Requires a SORTED array. n=1,000,000 → max 20 comparisons.
      function binarySearch(arr, target) {
        let left  = 0;
        let right = arr.length - 1;
      
        while (left <= right) {
          const mid = Math.floor((left + right) / 2); // find midpoint
      
          if      (arr[mid] === target) return mid;  // found it
          else if (arr[mid] <  target) left  = mid + 1; // target is right half
          else                           right = mid - 1; // target is left half
          // Each iteration: search space halves → log₂(n) steps total
        }
        return -1;
      }
TypeScript 
// ── O(1) — Constant Time ──────────────────────────────────
      function getFirst<T>(arr: T[]): T | undefined {
        return arr[0]; // 1 operation always
      }
      
      // ── O(n) — Linear Time ───────────────────────────────────
      function sumAll(arr: number[]): number {
        let total: number = 0;
        for (let i: number = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
          total += arr[i];
        }
        return total;
      }
      
      // ── O(n²) — Quadratic Time ───────────────────────────────
      function printPairs<T>(arr: T[]): void {
        for (let i: number = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
          for (let j: number = 0; j < arr.length; j++) {
            console.log(arr[i], arr[j]); // n × n = n² ops
          }
        }
      }
      
      // ── O(log n) — Logarithmic Time ──────────────────────────
      function binarySearch(arr: number[], target: number): number {
        let left:  number = 0;
        let right: number = arr.length - 1;
      
        while (left <= right) {
          const mid: number = Math.floor((left + right) / 2);
      
          if      (arr[mid] === target) return mid;
          else if (arr[mid] <  target) left  = mid + 1;
          else                           right = mid - 1;
        }
        return -1;
      }
      
      // TypeScript prevents passing unsorted arrays accidentally
      // by using opaque types (advanced pattern):
      type SortedArray = number[] & { readonly __sorted: true };

Big O Reference Table

NotationNameCommon Examplen = 100n = 1,000Rating
O(1) Constant Hash map lookup, array index 1 1 Excellent
O(log n) Logarithmic Binary search, balanced BST 7 10 Excellent
O(n) Linear Array scan, linear search 100 1,000 Good
O(n log n) Log-linear Merge sort, heap sort 664 9,966 Fair
O(n²) Quadratic Bubble sort, nested loops 10,000 1,000,000 Poor
O(2ⁿ) Exponential Recursive Fibonacci, subsets 10³⁰ 10³⁰¹ Unusable

Where You'll See This in the Real World

Search Engines

Google's inverted index enables O(1) keyword lookup across trillions of pages. Without it, every search would be O(n) over the entire web — physically impossible.

Large Dataset Sorting

JavaScript's Array.sort() uses TimSort — an O(n log n) hybrid algorithm. Choosing bubble sort instead for 1M records would be 150,000x slower.

Network Routing

BGP routing tables use prefix tries for O(log n) route lookups. Routers handle millions of packets per second — O(n) lookup per packet would collapse the internet.

DNA / Genomics Matching

Sequence alignment algorithms like Smith-Waterman are O(mn). Researchers optimize aggressively — naive O(n²) on a 3-billion-base genome would take centuries.

Lesson 05

Space vs Time

"Every algorithm consumes two resources: time (operations) and space (memory). Optimising one often costs the other — this is the fundamental trade-off of algorithm design."

Analogy

Two chefs prepare dinner. Chef A has memorised every recipe — ask them anything and they answer instantly, O(1). But their brain holds a finite amount. Chef B reads from a cookbook each time — no memory overhead, but every dish requires a full read, O(n). One trades memory for speed. The other trades speed for memory. Same kitchen, same menu, fundamentally different resource profiles.

The Trade-off Seesaw

fig. 05 — Two states of the time-space trade-off
Fig. 5 — Every optimisation is a seesaw. Pushing one side down always lifts the other.

The Two Dimensions of Every Algorithm

Time Complexity

Time complexity measures how the number of operations grows as the input size grows. It is what most engineers think of first when they say "Big O." An O(n) time complexity means the algorithm does approximately n operations. An O(n²) means roughly n² operations. Time is the resource that users notice directly — slow algorithms mean slow apps.

Space Complexity

Space complexity measures how much memory the algorithm allocates as the input size grows. Crucially, we only count the extra memory — not the input itself. If you pass in an array of 1,000 elements, that memory belongs to the caller, not the algorithm. Space complexity counts what your algorithm additionally creates: new arrays, hash maps, recursive call stack frames, auxiliary variables.

Key Insight — Auxiliary Space

When we say an algorithm has O(1) space complexity, we mean it uses a constant amount of extra memory — a few variables — regardless of input size. The input array itself is not counted. This distinction is called auxiliary space, and it is what space complexity always refers to.

The Trade-off in Practice

The most classic trade-off is memoisation: storing the result of expensive function calls so you never recompute them. A recursive Fibonacci function is O(2ⁿ) time and O(n) space. Add a cache (a hash map of previous results) and it becomes O(n) time and O(n) space. You spent memory to buy time — and the deal was worth it by several orders of magnitude.

The reverse trade-off appears in streaming algorithms. Processing a file too large to fit in memory means you must handle one chunk at a time — O(1) or O(k) space — but you cannot take shortcuts that require seeing the full dataset, so time suffers. Choosing which direction to optimise is a judgment call based on your system constraints.

When Memory Is the Bottleneck

On mobile devices, embedded systems, or services with strict memory limits, space complexity is not secondary to time complexity — it is often the primary constraint. A function that builds an O(n) lookup table might be the right answer on a server with 32GB RAM, and completely unacceptable on a device with 128MB. Good engineers always ask: what is the memory budget for this context?

Pseudocode — Same Problem, Two Trade-offs

Pseudocode
// APPROACH 1: Save memory, sacrifice time — O(n²) time, O(1) space FUNCTION hasDuplicateSlow(arr): FOR i FROM 0 TO len(arr): FOR j FROM i+1 TO len(arr): IF arr[i] == arr[j]: RETURN true RETURN false // No extra memory, but checks every pair → O(n²) // APPROACH 2: Spend memory, buy time — O(n) time, O(n) space FUNCTION hasDuplicateFast(arr): seen ← empty set // O(n) space — the trade-off cost FOR EACH item in arr: IF seen contains item: RETURN true // O(1) lookup every time ADD item to seen RETURN false // One pass, O(1) lookup → O(n) time

Implementation — hasDuplicate Two Ways

JavaScript 
// ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
      // VERSION 1 — O(n²) time | O(1) space
      // No extra memory. Compares every pair. Slow at scale.
      // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
      function hasDuplicateSlow(arr) {
        for (let i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {       // n iterations
          for (let j = i + 1; j < arr.length; j++) { // n-i iterations each
            if (arr[i] === arr[j]) return true;      // found a pair
          }
        }
        return false;
        // Space: O(1) — only i and j variables
        // Time:  O(n²) — n(n-1)/2 pairs checked
      }
      
      // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
      // VERSION 2 — O(n) time | O(n) space
      // Trade memory for speed. One pass. Fast at scale.
      // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
      function hasDuplicateFast(arr) {
        const seen = new Set();  // O(n) space — grows with input
      
        for (const item of arr) {
          if (seen.has(item)) return true; // O(1) lookup — hash set
          seen.add(item);
        }
        return false;
        // Space: O(n) — set stores up to n elements
        // Time:  O(n) — single pass, O(1) per lookup
      }
      
      // ── The trade-off in numbers ────────────────────────────
      const arr1k = Array.from({ length: 1000 }, (_, i) => i);
      
      // hasDuplicateSlow: ~500,000 comparisons
      // hasDuplicateFast: ~1,000 comparisons  ← 500x fewer
      
      // At n=100,000:
      // hasDuplicateSlow: ~5,000,000,000 comparisons (~5s)
      // hasDuplicateFast: ~100,000 comparisons        (~0.1ms)
TypeScript 
// ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
      // VERSION 1 — O(n²) time | O(1) space
      // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
      function hasDuplicateSlow<T>(arr: T[]): boolean {
        for (let i: number = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
          for (let j: number = i + 1; j < arr.length; j++) {
            if (arr[i] === arr[j]) return true;
          }
        }
        return false;
      }
      
      // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
      // VERSION 2 — O(n) time | O(n) space
      // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
      function hasDuplicateFast<T>(arr: T[]): boolean {
        const seen: Set<T> = new Set<T>();
      
        for (const item of arr) {
          if (seen.has(item)) return true;
          seen.add(item);
        }
        return false;
      }
      
      // ── Complexity comparison object ────────────────────────
      interface ComplexityProfile {
        time:  string;
        space: string;
        tradeoff: string;
      }
      
      const profiles: Record<string, ComplexityProfile> = {
        slow: { time: 'O(n²)', space: 'O(1)', tradeoff: 'memory-lean, slow' },
        fast: { time: 'O(n)',  space: 'O(n)', tradeoff: 'memory-heavy, fast' },
      };

Common Trade-off Patterns

PatternTimeSpaceWhen to use
Brute force O(n²) O(1) Tiny datasets, memory is severely constrained
Hash set / map cache O(n) O(n) General case — trading memory for speed is almost always worth it
Sorting + two pointers O(n log n) O(1) When sort cost is acceptable and memory is limited
Memoisation (top-down DP) O(n) O(n) Recursive problems with overlapping subproblems
Tabulation (bottom-up DP) O(n) O(1)–O(n) When you can reduce the table to a rolling window

Where You'll See This in the Real World

Redis Caching

Redis sits entirely in RAM — the ultimate space-for-time trade. A database query that takes 80ms becomes a 0.2ms cache hit. The cost: memory is expensive.

Browser localStorage

Browsers cache computed results (CSS layout, compiled JS) in localStorage to avoid recomputing. The trade: startup cost drops, disk usage rises.

CDN Edge Caching

CDNs store copies of content at edge nodes worldwide — massive space cost — so every user request is O(1) local retrieval instead of a cross-continental round trip.

Mobile Memory Limits

iOS terminates apps that consume too much RAM. Mobile algorithms often prefer O(n log n) time with O(1) space over O(n) time with O(n) space — the trade-off flips.

Lesson 06

Arrays

"An array is a contiguous block of memory storing elements of the same type — accessed in O(1) by index, but costly to resize or insert in the middle."

What is an Array?

An array stores items in consecutive memory slots. Because each slot is the same size, the computer can jump directly to any element using simple arithmetic: address = base + index × size. No searching required — that's why index access is always O(1).

Analogy

An array is like a numbered row of lockers. You know locker #47 is exactly 47 steps from the start — you don't check every locker to find it. That's what O(1) access feels like.

Insert & Delete — The Expensive Part

The price of contiguous memory: when you insert at position i, every element from i to the end must shift one slot right. In the worst case (inserting at index 0) that's n shifts — O(n).

Watch out

Arrays have a fixed capacity. When you "push" to a dynamic array (like JavaScript's) and it runs out of space, it secretly allocates a new array 2× the size and copies everything over — that copy is O(n).

Common Array Operations

JavaScript 
const arr = [14, 32, 7, 91, 55];
      
      // O(1) — direct index access
      const val = arr[2];                   // 7
      
      // O(1) amortised — push to end
      arr.push(99);                         // [14,32,7,91,55,99]
      
      // O(n) — insert at position 1 (shifts right)
      arr.splice(1, 0, 100);               // [14,100,32,7,91,55,99]
      
      // O(n) — remove from middle
      arr.splice(3, 1);                    // removes index 3
      
      // O(n) — linear search
      const idx = arr.indexOf(91);         // 3 (or -1 if not found)
      
      // O(n log n) — sort
      arr.sort((a, b) => a - b);           // ascending
TypeScript 
const arr: number[] = [14, 32, 7, 91, 55];
      
      // O(1) — direct index access
      const val: number = arr[2];            // 7
      
      // O(1) amortised — push to end
      arr.push(99);
      
      // O(n) — insert at middle
      arr.splice(1, 0, 100);
      
      // Type-safe search with find
      const found = arr.find((x) => x > 50); // first >50
      
      // Typed tuple for fixed-length arrays
      const pair: [string, number] = ['age', 30];
Python 
arr = [14, 32, 7, 91, 55]
      
      # O(1) — index access
      val = arr[2]                          # 7
      last = arr[-1]                        # 55 (negative index)
      
      # O(1) amortised — append to end
      arr.append(99)
      
      # O(n) — insert at position
      arr.insert(1, 100)
      
      # Slicing — O(k) where k = slice size
      sub = arr[1:4]                        # [100, 32, 7]
      
      # List comprehension — O(n)
      evens = [x for x in arr if x % 2 == 0]

Complexity Summary

Operation Time Space Notes
Access by indexO(1)O(1)base + offset arithmetic
Search (unsorted)O(n)O(1)must check every element
Search (sorted)O(log n)O(1)binary search
Insert at endO(1)*O(1)amortised; resize is O(n)
Insert at middleO(n)O(1)shift elements right
Delete at endO(1)O(1)just shrink length
Delete at middleO(n)O(1)shift elements left
SortO(n log n)O(log n)typical comparison sort

Where Arrays Show Up

Image pixels

Every image is a 2D array of pixels (RGBA values). GPU shaders iterate it in parallel — O(1) access per pixel is what makes real-time rendering possible.

Git history

Git stores commit lists as arrays (with linked pointers). Log traversal is O(n); accessing a specific commit by index in a reflog is O(1).

Database rows

SQL tables store rows in pages — essentially arrays on disk. Sequential scans are O(n); indexed lookups reduce it to O(log n) via B-tree.

Audio buffers

Audio samples are stored in Float32Arrays. Web Audio API's processing nodes read them frame-by-frame — contiguous layout ensures cache-friendly O(1) reads per sample.

Lesson 07

Strings

"A string is a sequence of characters — under the hood, an array of bytes. Immutability, encoding, and the hidden cost of concatenation define most string performance traps."

Strings Are Character Arrays

At the memory level, a string is just an array of character codes. "hello" is stored as five bytes: [104, 101, 108, 108, 111]. Every array operation from Lesson 06 applies — but most languages make strings immutable, meaning any "modification" creates a brand new string in memory.

Key insight

In Python and JavaScript, strings are immutable. Every + operator creates a brand new string. Concatenating n strings in a loop is O(n²) total — not O(n). Use array joins or string builders instead.

The Concatenation Trap

Each concatenation allocates a new string and copies both halves into it. Doing this n times means copying 1+2+3+…+n characters = O(n²) total work.

O(n²) — naive loop
  • String concat in a loop (s += word)
  • Each iteration copies all previous characters
  • Kills performance at n > 10,000
  • Common interview trap in Python/JS
O(n) — array join
  • Collect parts in array, call .join('') once
  • Single allocation for the final string
  • Python: ''.join(parts)
  • Java: StringBuilder.append()

String Patterns in Code

JavaScript 
// ── Common string operations ──────────────────────
      const s = 'Hello, World!';
      
      // O(1) — length, index access
      s.length;                            // 13
      s[0];                                // 'H'
      
      // O(n) — includes, indexOf
      s.includes('World');                // true
      s.indexOf('o');                     // 4
      
      // O(k) — slice, substring
      s.slice(7, 12);                      // 'World'
      
      // ── O(n²) trap ────────────────────────────────────
      let result = '';
      for (const word of words) {
        result += word + ' ';            // copies grow each time
      }
      
      // ── O(n) fix ──────────────────────────────────────
      const result2 = words.join(' ');    // single allocation
      
      // ── Reverse a string (interview classic) ──────────
      const reversed = s.split('').reverse().join(''); // O(n)
TypeScript 
// Check if string is palindrome — O(n)
      function isPalindrome(s: string): boolean {
        let left = 0, right = s.length - 1;
        while (left < right) {
          if (s[left] !== s[right]) return false;
          left++;
          right--;
        }
        return true;
      }
      
      // Count character frequency — O(n)
      function charFreq(s: string): Map<string, number> {
        const freq = new Map<string, number>();
        for (const ch of s) {
          freq.set(ch, (freq.get(ch) ?? 0) + 1);
        }
        return freq;
      }
      
      // Anagram check — O(n)
      function isAnagram(a: string, b: string): boolean {
        if (a.length !== b.length) return false;
        const freq = charFreq(a);
        for (const ch of b) {
          const count = freq.get(ch);
          if (!count) return false;
          freq.set(ch, count - 1);
        }
        return true;
      }
Python 
s = "Hello, World!"
      
      # O(1) — index access
      s[0]                              # 'H'
      s[-1]                             # '!'
      
      # O(k) — slice
      s[7:12]                           # 'World'
      
      # O(n) — reverse
      s[::-1]                           # '!dlroW ,olleH'
      
      # O(n²) trap — avoid this
      result = ""
      for word in words:
          result += word                 # copies grow
      
      # O(n) fix — join
      result = " ".join(words)           # single alloc
      
      # Counter for frequency map — O(n)
      from collections import Counter
      freq = Counter("banana")          # {'a':3,'n':2,'b':1}
      
      # Palindrome check — O(n)
      def is_palindrome(s):
          return s == s[::-1]

Complexity Summary

OperationTimeSpaceNotes
LengthO(1)O(1)stored as metadata
Index accessO(1)O(1)pointer + offset
Substring / sliceO(k)O(k)k = slice length
Search (contains)O(n)O(1)naive; KMP is O(n)
Concatenation (+)O(n)O(n)new allocation + copy
Concat in a loopO(n²)O(n)use join instead!
ReverseO(n)O(n)must create new string
SplitO(n)O(n)allocates array of parts

Where String Perf Matters

Search engines

Indexing and querying trillions of strings. Inverted indexes, prefix tries, and n-gram models all depend on efficient string operations to respond in milliseconds.

Security & hashing

Password hashing (bcrypt, Argon2) converts string inputs into fixed-length digests. Timing-safe comparison prevents leaking length information via early exit.

Code editors

VS Code uses a "piece tree" to represent file content as a series of string segments — avoiding O(n²) edits when you insert characters in large files.

DNS & routing

DNS lookups match domain strings using trie data structures. Prefix matching is O(m) where m is the domain length — independent of how many domains exist.

Lesson 08

Linked Lists

"A linked list is a chain of nodes where each node holds a value and a pointer to the next. Unlike arrays, nodes live anywhere in memory — giving O(1) insert/delete but O(n) access."

The Node — Building Block

Every linked list is made of nodes. Each node is a small object with two fields: a value (the data) and a next pointer (the memory address of the next node). The last node's next is null — that's the end of the list.

Analogy

A linked list is like a treasure hunt. Each clue (node) tells you the location of the next clue (next pointer). To find clue #5, you must follow clues 1→2→3→4 first. That's why access is O(n), not O(1).

Singly vs Doubly Linked

A singly linked list has one pointer per node (next). A doubly linked list adds a prev pointer — doubling memory per node, but enabling O(1) backward traversal and making deletion easier when you already have a reference to the node.

Singly Linked
  • One pointer per node (next)
  • Less memory overhead
  • Deletion requires previous node reference
  • Forward traversal only
  • Stack, simple queues
Doubly Linked
  • Two pointers per node (prev + next)
  • 2× memory per node
  • O(1) deletion with just a node reference
  • Forward and backward traversal
  • Deque, browser history, LRU cache

O(1) Insert — Why It's Fast

Inserting a new node between two existing nodes only requires re-wiring two pointers — no shifting of elements like arrays. The catch: you first need a pointer to the node before the insertion point, which takes O(n) to find by traversal.

Interview tip

When asked "insert a node in O(1)", always clarify: O(1) requires already having a pointer to the predecessor node. Finding that node first is O(n). The insert itself — the pointer rewiring — is O(1).

Implementing a Linked List

JavaScript 
class Node {
        constructor(val) {
          this.val  = val;
          this.next = null;
        }
      }
      
      class LinkedList {
        constructor() {
          this.head = null;
          this.size = 0;
        }
      
        // O(1) — prepend to front
        prepend(val) {
          const node = new Node(val);
          node.next  = this.head;
          this.head  = node;
          this.size++;
        }
      
        // O(n) — traverse and find
        find(val) {
          let curr = this.head;
          while (curr) {
            if (curr.val === val) return curr;
            curr = curr.next;
          }
          return null;
        }
      
        // O(n) — delete by value
        delete(val) {
          if (!this.head) return;
          if (this.head.val === val) {
            this.head = this.head.next;
            this.size--;
            return;
          }
          let prev = this.head;
          while (prev.next) {
            if (prev.next.val === val) {
              prev.next = prev.next.next;  // skip over it
              this.size--;
              return;
            }
            prev = prev.next;
          }
        }
      }
TypeScript 
class ListNode<T> {
        val:  T;
        next: ListNode<T> | null = null;
        constructor(val: T) { this.val = val; }
      }
      
      class LinkedList<T> {
        private head: ListNode<T> | null = null;
        private size: number = 0;
      
        prepend(val: T): void {
          const node = new ListNode(val);
          node.next  = this.head;
          this.head  = node;
          this.size++;
        }
      
        toArray(): T[] {
          const result: T[] = [];
          let curr = this.head;
          while (curr) {
            result.push(curr.val);
            curr = curr.next;
          }
          return result;
        }
      
        // Reverse in-place — O(n) time, O(1) space
        reverse(): void {
          let prev: ListNode<T> | null = null;
          let curr = this.head;
          while (curr) {
            const next = curr.next;
            curr.next  = prev;
            prev       = curr;
            curr       = next;
          }
          this.head = prev;
        }
      }
Python 
class Node:
          def __init__(self, val):
              self.val  = val
              self.next = None
      
      class LinkedList:
          def __init__(self):
              self.head = None
      
          # O(1) prepend
          def prepend(self, val):
              node      = Node(val)
              node.next = self.head
              self.head = node
      
          # O(n) traversal
          def to_list(self):
              result, curr = [], self.head
              while curr:
                  result.append(curr.val)
                  curr = curr.next
              return result
      
          # O(n) — detect cycle (Floyd's algorithm)
          def has_cycle(self):
              slow = fast = self.head
              while fast and fast.next:
                  slow = slow.next
                  fast = fast.next.next
                  if slow is fast:
                      return True
              return False

Complexity: Array vs Linked List

OperationArrayLinked ListWinner
Access by indexO(1)O(n)Array
SearchO(n)O(n)Tie
Insert at frontO(n)O(1)Linked List
Insert at middleO(n)O(1)*Linked List*
Insert at endO(1)*O(n)Array*
Delete at frontO(n)O(1)Linked List
Memory layoutArray: contiguous  |  Linked: scattered (pointer overhead)Array (cache)

* Insert at middle requires finding the predecessor first (O(n) traversal). Insert marked O(1) assumes pointer already held. Array end-insert is amortised O(1).

Real-World Uses

Browser history

Each page visit is a node in a doubly linked list. Back/forward buttons are O(1) pointer moves — no shifting, no copying.

Text editor undo

Undo/redo stacks are often linked lists of "change records." Each undo is an O(1) pointer follow — the prior state doesn't need to be recomputed.

OS memory allocator

Free memory blocks are tracked in a linked list. When you call malloc, the allocator finds a suitable block in O(n) and unlinks it in O(1).

LRU Cache

A doubly linked list + hash map implements an LRU cache with O(1) get and put. The list tracks recency; the map gives O(1) node access for relinking.

Lesson 09

Stacks

"A stack is a Last-In, First-Out (LIFO) structure — like a stack of plates. You can only add or remove from the top. Both operations are O(1)."

LIFO — Last In, First Out

A stack enforces a strict access rule: the last item pushed is the first item popped. Think of a stack of plates — you can only take from the top. This constraint is actually a superpower: it makes stacks perfect for tracking "what came most recently."

Analogy

A stack of plates at a restaurant. The chef places clean plates on top (push). Servers always take from the top (pop). A plate on the bottom might wait a long time — it was the first in, but last out.

The Call Stack — Stacks in Your Runtime

Every time your code calls a function, the runtime pushes a stack frame onto the call stack. When the function returns, its frame is popped. This is why recursive functions can cause a "stack overflow" — too many nested calls exhaust the stack's fixed memory.

Watch out

Infinite recursion without a base case will overflow the call stack. JavaScript throws RangeError: Maximum call stack size exceeded. Always define your base case first — it's the equivalent of the null at the bottom of the stack.

Implementing a Stack

JavaScript 
// Stack backed by an array — all ops O(1)
      class Stack {
        constructor() { this.data = []; }
      
        push(item) { this.data.push(item); }          // O(1)
        pop()       { return this.data.pop(); }        // O(1)
        peek()      { return this.data.at(-1); }      // O(1)
        isEmpty()   { return this.data.length === 0; }
        get size()  { return this.data.length; }
      }
      
      // ── Classic use: balanced brackets ─────────────────
      function isBalanced(s) {
        const stack = new Stack();
        const pairs = { ')':'(', ']':'[', '}':'{' };
      
        for (const ch of s) {
          if ('([{'.includes(ch))  { stack.push(ch); }
          else if (ch in pairs) {
            if (stack.pop() !== pairs[ch]) return false;
          }
        }
        return stack.isEmpty();
      }
      
      isBalanced('({[]})');  // true
      isBalanced('([)');     // false
TypeScript 
class Stack<T> {
        private data: T[] = [];
      
        push(item: T): void       { this.data.push(item); }
        pop(): T | undefined    { return this.data.pop(); }
        peek(): T | undefined   { return this.data.at(-1); }
        isEmpty(): boolean      { return this.data.length === 0; }
        get size(): number      { return this.data.length; }
      }
      
      // Evaluate postfix expression — O(n)
      function evalPostfix(tokens: string[]): number {
        const stack = new Stack<number>();
        for (const tok of tokens) {
          if (!isNaN(Number(tok))) {
            stack.push(Number(tok));
          } else {
            const b = stack.pop()!;
            const a = stack.pop()!;
            if      (tok === '+') stack.push(a + b);
            else if (tok === '-') stack.push(a - b);
            else if (tok === '*') stack.push(a * b);
            else                   stack.push(a / b);
          }
        }
        return stack.pop()!;
      }
      // ["3","4","+","2","*"] → (3+4)*2 = 14
Python 
# Python list as a stack — O(1) append/pop from end
      stack = []
      stack.append(10)         # push
      stack.append(25)
      top = stack.pop()         # pop  → 25
      peek = stack[-1]         # peek → 10
      
      # DFS with an explicit stack (avoids recursion limit)
      def dfs_iterative(graph, start):
          visited = set()
          stack   = [start]
          while stack:
              node = stack.pop()
              if node in visited:
                  continue
              visited.add(node)
              for neighbour in graph[node]:
                  if neighbour not in visited:
                      stack.append(neighbour)
          return visited
      
      # Balanced parentheses — O(n)
      def is_balanced(s):
          stack, pairs = [], {')':'(', ']':'[', '}':'{'}
          for ch in s:
              if ch in '([{': stack.append(ch)
              elif ch in pairs:
                  if not stack or stack.pop() != pairs[ch]:
                      return False
          return not stack

Complexity Summary

OperationTimeSpaceNotes
pushO(1)O(1)add to top
popO(1)O(1)remove from top
peek / topO(1)O(1)read top without removing
isEmptyO(1)O(1)check size === 0
SearchO(n)O(1)must pop to search
Space (n items)—O(n)one slot per item

Where Stacks Show Up

Function call stack

Every language runtime uses a stack to track function calls, local variables, and return addresses. It's why recursion works — and why it breaks when too deep.

Ctrl+Z (Undo)

Every keystroke is pushed onto an undo stack. Ctrl+Z pops the last action and reverses it. Ctrl+Y pushes it onto a redo stack — two stacks, infinite undo/redo.

Git stash

git stash is literally a stack. Each git stash push saves your current changes on top. git stash pop restores the most recent — LIFO behaviour built into version control.

Expression parsers

Compilers and calculators use stacks to parse and evaluate expressions — matching brackets, handling operator precedence, and converting infix to postfix notation.

Lesson 10

Queues

"A queue is a First-In, First-Out (FIFO) structure — like a line at a coffee shop. Items enter at the back and leave from the front. Enqueue and dequeue are both O(1)."

FIFO — First In, First Out

A queue is the opposite of a stack. Items join at the back (enqueue) and leave from the front (dequeue). The first item added is always the first served — fair ordering is the queue's defining property.

Analogy

A queue is the coffee shop line. The first person in line is served first. A new customer joins at the back. Nobody cuts in — that's what makes it fair and predictable.

Queue vs Stack — Same Tools, Opposite Order

Queues and stacks both support O(1) insert and remove — but serve completely different ordering guarantees. The choice between them determines whether you process things in arrival order (queue) or reverse order (stack).

Queue (FIFO)
  • First in = first out
  • Enqueue at back, dequeue from front
  • Fair ordering — arrival order preserved
  • BFS traversal, task schedulers, print queues
  • Python: collections.deque
Stack (LIFO)
  • Last in = first out
  • Push and pop from same end (top)
  • Reverse ordering — most recent first
  • DFS traversal, undo/redo, call stack
  • Python: plain list with append/pop

BFS — The Classic Queue Algorithm

Breadth-First Search explores a graph level by level — all nodes at distance 1, then distance 2, and so on. A queue is the natural fit: enqueue neighbours, process them in arrival order. This guarantees the shortest path in an unweighted graph.

Key insight

BFS with a queue guarantees shortest path in an unweighted graph because it exhausts all paths of length k before exploring length k+1. Swap the queue for a stack and you get DFS — which explores as deep as possible first.

Implementing a Queue

JavaScript 
// Naive array queue — dequeue is O(n) due to shift()
      class NaiveQueue {
        constructor() { this.data = []; }
        enqueue(item) { this.data.push(item); }    // O(1)
        dequeue()     { return this.data.shift(); } // O(n) ← problem!
      }
      
      // O(1) dequeue — use a pointer instead of shifting
      class Queue {
        constructor() { this.data = []; this.head = 0; }
      
        enqueue(item) { this.data.push(item); }      // O(1)
      
        dequeue() {                                   // O(1)
          if (this.isEmpty()) return undefined;
          return this.data[this.head++];
        }
      
        peek()    { return this.data[this.head]; }
        isEmpty() { return this.head >= this.data.length; }
        get size(){ return this.data.length - this.head; }
      }
      
      // BFS using queue — O(V + E)
      function bfs(graph, start) {
        const visited = new Set([start]);
        const queue   = new Queue();
        queue.enqueue(start);
        while (!queue.isEmpty()) {
          const node = queue.dequeue();
          for (const nb of graph[node] || []) {
            if (!visited.has(nb)) { visited.add(nb); queue.enqueue(nb); }
          }
        }
        return [...visited];
      }
TypeScript 
class Queue<T> {
        private data: T[] = [];
        private head: number = 0;
      
        enqueue(item: T): void      { this.data.push(item); }
      
        dequeue(): T | undefined  {
          if (this.isEmpty()) return undefined;
          return this.data[this.head++];
        }
      
        peek(): T | undefined     { return this.data[this.head]; }
        isEmpty(): boolean        { return this.head >= this.data.length; }
        get size(): number        { return this.data.length - this.head; }
      }
      
      // Shortest path in unweighted graph — O(V + E)
      function shortestPath(
        graph: Record<string, string[]>,
        start: string,
        end: string
      ): string[] | null {
        const queue = new Queue<string[]>();
        const visited = new Set([start]);
        queue.enqueue([start]);
        while (!queue.isEmpty()) {
          const path = queue.dequeue()!;
          const node = path[path.length - 1];
          if (node === end) return path;
          for (const nb of graph[node] ?? []) {
            if (!visited.has(nb)) {
              visited.add(nb);
              queue.enqueue([...path, nb]);
            }
          }
        }
        return null;
      }
Python 
from collections import deque
      
      # deque provides O(1) append (enqueue) and popleft (dequeue)
      q = deque()
      q.append('A')          # enqueue
      q.append('B')
      first = q.popleft()   # dequeue → 'A'  O(1)
      
      # BFS — shortest path in unweighted graph
      def bfs(graph, start, end):
          visited = {start}
          queue   = deque([[start]])
          while queue:
              path = queue.popleft()
              node = path[-1]
              if node == end:
                  return path
              for nb in graph.get(node, []):
                  if nb not in visited:
                      visited.add(nb)
                      queue.append(path + [nb])
          return None
      
      # Priority queue (min-heap) — Dijkstra's algorithm
      import heapq
      pq = []
      heapq.heappush(pq, (0, 'A'))   # (priority, item)
      cost, node = heapq.heappop(pq)  # O(log n)

Complexity Summary

OperationTimeSpaceNotes
enqueueO(1)O(1)add to back
dequeueO(1)O(1)remove from front (pointer trick)
peek / frontO(1)O(1)read without removing
isEmptyO(1)O(1)head >= length
dequeue via shift()O(n)O(1)naive array — avoid this
Space (n items)—O(n)one slot per queued item

Where Queues Show Up

CPU task scheduler

Operating systems use queues (often priority queues) to schedule processes. Each CPU core processes tasks in order, ensuring no process is starved indefinitely.

Message queues

Kafka, RabbitMQ, and SQS are industrial-scale queues. Producers enqueue messages; consumers dequeue at their own pace — decoupling services and absorbing traffic spikes.

Network packets

Routers buffer incoming packets in queues. When bandwidth is limited, packets wait their turn — the Internet is largely built on FIFO queues at every hop.

Notification delivery

Push notifications (iOS APNs, FCM) flow through message queues. Each notification enqueues to ensure delivery order and retry logic without overwhelming the device.

Course complete

You've covered all 10 foundations — DSA, Complexity, Big O, Space vs Time, Arrays, Strings, Linked Lists, Stacks, and Queues. These are the building blocks every engineer reaches for daily. The patterns you've learned here — O(1) vs O(n), contiguous vs pointer-linked, LIFO vs FIFO — will surface in every data structure and algorithm you encounter from here on.
